  • Investors with diversified, quality-tilted portfolios will be well-positioned in the new year.
  • Not every company will ride the AI wave; there will be winners and losers, and higher quality companies that can justify valuations will be recognized as better investments.
  • After languishing in a risk-on environment, quality stocks are trading below historical valuation levels—and are poised for a rebound.

After flirting with bear territory, stocks rose to new highs in 2025, driven by traders’ optimism over artificial intelligence and hope that Fed cuts will continue to power the economy and corporate profits. Still, the big question on everyone’s minds is whether the trillions of dollars pouring into AI will yield a return on investment. Furthermore, 10-year Treasury-bond yields have risen to highs last seen in 2009, signaling that the rate-cutting cycle may be nearing an end amid sticky inflation and an accelerating budget deficit. This creates challenges for investors in 2026 and likely increases market volatility. Investors with diversified portfolios with a quality tilt will be well-positioned in the new year.

Parts of the economy were strong in 2025, such as semiconductors, data centers, and affluent consumers. But other parts of the economy, such as housing, manufacturing, and lower-income spending, were either flat or in contraction. According to the National Bureau of Economic Research data, it takes roughly 10 months to reset after a downturn. As such, it is likely that economic growth will broaden to some of the struggling pockets of the economy in 2026. Diversified portfolios stand to benefit if consumer staples, real estate, and materials stocks drive market returns.

The AI rally broadened, and bubble fears became more pronounced throughout 2025. Investors debated the disruption the technology represents and whether there is a bubble in AI-related stocks. There is no doubt that AI is having a profound impact on the world, but like the dotcom era, there will be winners and losers. Furthermore, a large concentration of companies that trade above 10x sales is a risky portfolio. Against this backdrop, we believe it’s best to identify high-quality companies that can effectively harness this technology to the benefit of stakeholders and have justified valuation multiples.

Low-quality unprofitable tech stocks surged in 2025, significantly outperforming quality stocks. The chart below illustrates investor enthusiasm for young unprofitable companies (YUCs) relative to companies that make money and generate tangible returns on investments.

Companies with Negative Earnings Outperformed Companies with Positive Earnings

In 2025, quality stocks posted their worst performance since the dotcom era. This is largely because an unusually high number of high-quality stocks lack the strong momentum attributes that are favored in the current risk-on market. Quality stocks—companies with high returns on equity, industry-leading margins, and stable earnings growth—are trading below their historical valuation levels, while the overall market is expensive. Typically, quality stocks deliver strong relative returns after such underperformance. Now is the time to invest in quality!

High-quality Stocks’ Underperformance Is at 1999 Extremes

Footnotes

1The Trump administration's housing policy plans for 2026 involve a two-pronged approach focusing on both affordability through lower interest rates and increased supply through deregulation.

2Margin debt for US equities now stands near a record $1.1 trillion and its cost of carry has been rising.

3High volume around the 6000 threshold for the S&P 500 implies or constitutes a natural zone of support in the event of a panic sell-off.

4For the year ending 12/31/2025, the large cap Russell 1000 Index gained 17.37%, and the small cap Russell 2000 Index gained 12.81%. For the three months ending 12/31/2025, the Russell 1000 Index gained 2.41%, and the Russell 2000 Index gained 2.19%.

* For the one-year time horizon (1/1/25 to 12/31/25), the 52-day daily realized volatility metric of CMNIX was 1.473 versus 2.932 for the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Source: Bloomberg. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The 52-day daily realized volatility metric measures the fluctuation (standard deviation of returns, annualized) in an asset’s price over the past 52 trading days, using daily return data.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LGI Homes Has A 'Lot' Of Problems (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The last several months have been a brutal time for shareholders of LGI Homes (LGIH). Shares of the homebuilder have plummeted 28.9% since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate back in March of 2025. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 22.1%. This performance disparity can be attributed to a couple of things. First and foremost, we have a tough housing market. Homebuilding activity has dropped because of home affordability issues, which I have detailed in other articles. This includes high prices from prior years, combined with high-interest rates aimed at combating inflationary pressures. Tariffs today most certainly are not helping the situation any. And with the US likely to enter into a recession before long, the prospect of additional weakness impacting the company should be considered.

Another issue relates to the large and growing debt balance that the company has. Management has continued to take on debt in order to maintain a large lot position in a rapidly inflating environment. This is more problematic, in my opinion, than broader market conditions. Economic weakness eventually subsides. But structural problems caused by management can stick around for a while. Add on top of this how expensive the stock is on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, and even though there have been some developments recently, with the stock jumping 13.9% on January 9th in response to movement in the political sphere, I believe that downgrading it to a soft ‘sell’ is the appropriate response.

A risky place for your money

A lot can happen in less than a year. The US went from having some housing related issues but being fundamentally sound to experiencing meaningful weakness. The housing market has taken a huge hit, and the consumer is showing signs of weakness that need to be monitored closely. In few places have we seen issues as severe as what we are seeing in the housing market. When it comes to a homebuilder such as LGI Homes, the data looks outright scary. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2024.

Revenue has dropped 39.2% year over year, dropping from $651.9 million to $396.6 million. This was the result of a plunge in homes closed from 1,757 to 1,065. The picture would have been worse had it not been for the fact that the average price of a home closed actually inched up from $371,004 to $372,424. Many home builders have purposely offered larger incentives aimed at luring buyers in. Clearly, LGI Homes has taken a different approach.

This does not mean that there hasn't been some regional variance. In the image above, you can see average selling prices for homes based on the different regions in which the company operates. In some of the hotter markets, like parts of the West, the Southeast, and specifically Florida, prices rose nicely. These are markets where demand is unusually strong. This has allowed the company to actually provide fewer incentives since demand here is more robust than it is in some other parts of the country, like the Northeast, where I live. The big pain point for the company involved the Northwest, with the average price plummeting 18.1% year over year.

As revenue fell, net profits dropped also. The company went from a net income of $69.6 million to $19.7 million. Operating cash flow did improve from negative $17.7 million to negative $13.2 million. But when we strip out changes in working capital, we get a drop from $72.2 million to $21.3 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted sharply from $105.6 million to $38.9 million. In the chart above, you can also see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, were all lower here. And they can be attributed to the decline in the number of homes closed.

As bad as this data looks, what I can tell you is that there is some positive news on the horizon. For starters, we do not have quarterly cancellation rate data. But management does provide this metric for the first nine months of 2025 in its entirety. Despite this number increasing from an already high 21.6% to 28.1%, we have started to see a turnaround in net new orders. The firm actually reported net new orders of 1,570 homes during the third quarter. That's up from the 1,452 homes ordered the previous year. Having said that, as the chart above illustrates, we are still down for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024.

Backlog data is also promising. At the end of the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, LGI Homes had a backlog of 1,088 homes. But today, that stands at 1,305 homes. Any sort of growth here is positive, as it indicates that future revenue should be higher, keeping all else the same. On top of this, we have seen a bit of resilience when it comes to home closings for the final quarter of the year. In October of last year, the 395 homes closed ended up being 24.8% lower than the 525 supported the previous year. In November, Closings were down 25%. But in December, the drop year over year was only 1.9%. That's with 569 homes closed, and when combined with the other months leading up to that point, translates to 4,788 homes for the year. That's still down meaningfully from the previous year. But the fact that we saw any sort of stability on that front is promising.

We don't really know what to expect when it comes to the rest of the 2025 fiscal year. But if we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would get net profits of $74.6 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $83.9 million, and EBITDA of $147.2 million. Using these estimates, as well as the historical results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart below how the stock is priced. These multiples, sadly, don't put the company in the undervalued category. In fact, they mostly put it near the upper end of the fair value range. This would typically be enough to justify a cautious ‘hold’ rating. But there are other considerations as well. For starters, in that same chart, you can see how the company stacks up against five other similar firms. Although it is decently priced on a relative basis when it comes to the price-to-operating cash flow approach, it is relatively expensive when it comes to the other two approaches.

In the chart below, you can see the net leverage ratio of the company using projected results for the 2025 fiscal year. The reading of 11.48 puts the firm well above four of the five homebuilders that I compared it to already. This is problematic in and of itself. And it most certainly deserves a good bit of unpacking. After all, in my early days investing, I found out the hard way what happens when you buy into companies that have way too much leverage. Even when they look cheap, and this one doesn't, the end result can be a great deal of pain.

To effectively analyze the overall financial picture here, I first wanted to see what has happened with the company's balance sheet relative to its cash flows in recent years. In the chart below, you can see net debt, EBITDA, and the net leverage ratio for the 2022 fiscal year through the first nine months of 2025. We have seen an explosion of leverage. And honestly, when we see something like that happen, oftentimes it is a misallocation of capital by management. That typically takes the form of excessive share buybacks and/or dividends.

Interestingly, however, that has not been the case. During this window of time, the company paid nothing in the way of dividends. And stock buybacks have been quite small. The firm bought $95.1 million worth of stock in 2022. It bought nothing in 2023. And from the start of 2024 through the end of the third quarter of last year, total share buybacks have amounted to only $54.6 million. Most obviously, these activities are not the problem.

When I dug deeper, I found that the biggest issue was an explosion in the real estate inventory that the company has on hand. This takes multiple forms. In the chart below, you can see the composition of real estate that the company has had over this window of time, and how it has expanded. Some of this can be attributed to completed homes and homes in progress. In fact, I would say that those increases were not immaterial. The fact of the matter is that, because of how long homes take to build and how reporting works from a schedule perspective, you will end up with some homes in inventory. And even though we have seen a drop in the number of homes closed since the end of 2023, the fact that home prices increased years ago certainly inflated these results.

The bigger problem relates to land, land under development, and finished lots. What's really intriguing here is that this occurred in spite of the fact that the number of lots that the company has actually declined in recent years. After seeing an increase in lots controlled through options contracts from 13,184 in 2022 to 17,582 in 2024, the number ultimately declined to only 9,416 lots in the third quarter of 2025. But honestly, this change should not be massive. After all, there's a big difference in cost between lots controlled and lots owned. And here, we do see a consistent drop from 58,720 lots in 2022 to 53,148 now. A couple of things can contribute to this. For starters, transitioning toward lots that are finished or close to finished is expensive. This ultimately speeds up the time for delivering a home and reduces development costs. But any change in the composition of lots can have a negative impact on a business because of how much it must pay for that extra value. Focusing on high demand property is also potentially problematic, especially during a time when land prices increased because of a growth in the demand for housing.

The good news is that, in theory, management can sell off these lots. And it seems as though they are open to that. During their third quarter earnings call, the company mentioned that they are actively working on monetizing some of their finished lots. But some of them are being held in the queue for future home construction. If the firm could sell off enough of its lots at reasonable prices, which might be harder in this environment considering the pain that homebuilders are seeing because of a decline in homebuying activity, that could conceivably be reduced to a considerable degree. This is not the only change that management is making. There is the possibility that the strength seen in December of 2025 when it came to closings will be part of a new trend for the company. Management mentioned in their final quarter earnings call, for instance, that they are pushing new financing incentives aimed at boosting home buying activity. This would certainly bode well if it continues. And it could help to accelerate the monetization of lots.

In the long run, I am a believer in the homebuilding industry. By some estimates, there are 4.7 million fewer homes in the US than what is needed. 8.1 million families live with somebody that they are not related to. Even though we are heading into a recession in my opinion, interest rates are falling, and any sort of decline in the economy would cause interest rates to be slashed more rapidly. All of these bode well, in the long run, for strong homebuying demand.

Despite there being certain headwinds that the industry is facing, such as a massive and persistent home affordability gap, there is also the potential for political developments. The whole reason why shares of LGI Homes rose so much on January 9th stems from news developing that Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick ended up meeting with executives from major companies in this space. Lately, Donald Trump has been conveying the idea of making life more affordable for everyday Americans. While this is completely antithetical to the trade war situation that was started, it is obviously something that makes for a good political push as we head into midterm elections. In addition to putting the Federal Reserve on blast multiple times in the past, effectively demanding that they lower interest rates faster, he also came out this past week and promised a ban on institutional investors that would result in them being unable to buy single-family homes. He also directed Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy $200 billion in mortgage bonds in an effort to push interest rates lower without the aid of the Federal Reserve.

When you add these two developments occurring in such a short window of time to the fact that Howard Lutnick decided to meet with major industry executives, there is the possibility that some sort of bill could come out of all of this. One source quoted Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte as saying that, “Builders who do business with Fannie and Freddie (which is most builders) need to start building out their lot supply, including option land, which is ‘ready to go.’ More to come on our actions we will take’. He also said that, “We are looking at everything and anything that can bring down the cost of homes,” while emphasizing that the goal is to also push companies to build more homes.

There definitely are some things in the political sphere that could serve as levers to get homes being built again in larger numbers. Examples would be tax benefits, government stimulus, the cutting of certain regulations, and more. It would be speculative, of course, to guess what might ultimately occur, if anything. And, in the political arena, even on an issue that most politicians would likely agree on, optics are everything, and, heading into the midterm season, getting much passed through Congress might be a herculean endeavor.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I believe that while LGI Homes does have some potential, the company is also risky right now. The stock is expensive, and leverage is much higher than I would like it to be. If something big develops on the political side, a more neutral or even bullish stance could be warranted. Right now, however, I would say that there are much more attractive opportunities that can be had. And that is why I believe that downgrading LGI Homes to a ‘sell’ makes the most sense.

data.title: Genmab: AI Partnership And Clinical Data Fuel Growth
data.description:
While Genmab (GMAB) continues to generate an immense amount of revenue from Darzalex and other royalties, my review of Genmab’s most recent financial performance and strategic actions over the last year makes me think that we are beginning to witness a fundamental transformation in how the market views Genmab. Genmab is no longer simply a technology licensor, but is rapidly developing into a full-fledged innovation powerhouse with a proprietary pipeline that the market has yet to fully recognize.

Balance Sheet Driving Growth

Results for the first nine months of 2025 financially show a company that has been able to grow top-line revenue aggressively with great financial discipline. Top-line revenue grew to $2.66 billion, representing a significant 21% increase compared to the same time frame last year. Double-digit revenue growth is impressive when you consider it is coming from high-quality, recurring revenue streams. Royalty revenue alone was $2.22 billion, a 23% increase from last year. Royalties are particularly important for Genmab as they represent a large majority of their overall revenue and are one of the key factors that contribute to their success. Royalties have also become a strong source of non-dilutive funding for Genmab as they continue to grow and pursue other opportunities.

One of the most compelling metrics in the report is operating profit, which rose to $1 billion for the nine month period, a huge improvement from $662 million reported in the prior year. Operating profit was a 52% improvement from last year's performance, a significant improvement compared to the 21% revenue growth. The significant operating leverage demonstrated in the report indicates that Genmab is efficiently scaling their business; they are continuing to build out their pipeline and expand their capabilities without costs increasing disproportionately.

Genmab is also well positioned in terms of their capital structure. According to the company's most recent investor presentation, Genmab currently has a cash position of approximately $3.4 billion. This large cash position provides a means to strategically deploy funds toward the pursuit of acquisitions, such as the proposed acquisition of Merus, and to support the expansion of the Rina-S program.

The Pipeline: Shifting to Ownership

The company is aggressively pursuing projects where they own at least 50% of the rights and therefore shift the economic benefits of those projects from mid-teen royalty rates to either profit sharing or full ownership. The crown jewel of this effort currently is Epkinly (epcoritamab), a bispecific antibody that will become a foundational therapy for many B-cell malignancy patients.

Although Epkinly has received approval for specific lines of treatment for both Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma and Follicular Lymphoma, I believe the opportunity to expand the use of Epkinly is where the true value exists. In a Phase 3 EPCORE FL-1 trial, the company reported positive dual primary endpoints of Overall Response Rate and Progression-Free Survival with statistical significance. The company reported that the risk of disease progression or death is reduced by 79%, which is a level of benefit that is hard to ignore in the oncology space.

In addition to Epkinly, I believe attention is now focused on Rina-S (Rinatabart Sesutecan), a new antibody-drug conjugate that targets Folate Receptor alpha. Genmab owns all of the economics associated with Rina-S, which means that they receive all of the upside on this project. As previously mentioned, the FDA has already granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation for Rina-S in advanced endometrial cancer. Data recently presented at major medical conferences demonstrated encouraging confirmation of the overall response rate for Rina-S and a safety profile that did not include the ocular toxicity issues that have plagued other ADCs in this class. The development of Rina-S demonstrates the next leg of growth for Genmab and supports the company's ability to develop complex ADCs in-house.

Significance of Recent Clinical Data

The data reported at ASH is quite impressive. In the EPCORE CLL-1 trial, patients treated with epcoritamab monotherapy in the first-line setting were found to have an Overall Response Rate of 57 percent, with 52 percent of those patients having a complete response. It is significant to note that a monotherapy had a complete response in more than half of the patients in such a difficult-to-treat indication. Additionally, in the combination arm with lenalidomide the overall response rate increased to 82 percent with a complete response rate of 73 percent.

While RT represents a much smaller patient population relative to DLBCL or FL; however, this success is broadening in scope for the stock. Specifically, it is providing the halo effect of the mechanism of action - if it is effective in the aggressive, refractory biology of Richter's, confidence will increase for its effectiveness in other difficult to treat B-cell malignancies. Additionally, Genmab will have a niche market in which they will have pricing power and rapid adoption when approved due to the lack of a standard of care. Lastly, the safety profile of epcoritamab was consistent with all prior trials, with manageable cytokine release syndrome indicating that this drug can be safely administered to fragile patient populations. These results are solidifying Epkinly as not only a product, but also as a pipeline in a product with the ability to generate revenue streams through multiple distinct indications.

The Anthropic AI Partnership

Genmab just recently announced a partnership with Anthropic to implement Claude-powered agentic AI solutions into their R&D processes. Although Genmab will likely be one of many companies issuing an AI-press release as part of a hype cycle, there appear to be some key differences between this announcement and others. The focus of this partnership is specific to the deployment of Claude-powered agentic AI solutions in the field of clinical development. The importance of this partnership is in increasing the operational efficiency of Genmab's R&D process.

Clinical trials are typically large and complex datasets requiring a great deal of documentation. The majority of the time spent in the development of drugs is not spent conducting the actual science behind it; rather, it is spent processing and analyzing the data (including documents) and generating the regulatory documentation necessary to advance the drug through Phase 2 and into Phase 3 and ultimately to FDA approval. Therefore, by using Anthropic's state-of-the-art models to automate and speed-up the processing of data, Genmab is addressing what is currently the largest cost center and the most significant time-sink in the biotechnology industry.

If this partnership enables Genmab to shorten their clinical timelines by just a few months, the financial benefits will be substantial. For example, an additional quarter of patent exclusivity on a drug like Epkinly or Rina-S could result in hundreds of millions of dollars in returns. The partnership signifies that Genmab has taken steps to modernize its supporting infrastructure to accommodate the size of a major pharmaceutical company while avoiding the administrative overhead typical of large-scale companies.

Counterarguments and Risk Factors

The largest counterpoint to the long thesis is that Genmab is still relying heavily on the income from its partners. Although Genmab is diversifying, a large portion of their current cash flow is still coming from Darzalex royalties through J&J (JNJ). If a competitor could supplant Darzalex as the gold standard for treatment in multiple myeloma or Genmab experienced some unanticipated legal problems related to the patents, Genmab's entire source of income would be severely disrupted. Even though the patent cliff is a ways away, any disruption to this cash cow is a serious disruption to the engine of research and development funding.

Genmab also faces the risk of executing the transition to a wholly owned model. To develop, manufacture, and commercially sell a drug independently is far more complicated and costly than developing a drug and then licensing it to another party after successful completion of phase one clinical trials. As Genmab assumes greater responsibility for these functions—and assumes additional responsibilities such as integrating a potential acquisition, such as Merus—Genmab's operating expenses will likely increase, which could result in margin compression in the short term. The market may punish Genmab if the cost increases faster than the proprietary revenue.

TAM and Competitive Landscape

The B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma market is large. In the U.S., Japan, and EU5, there are more than 100,000 annual patients for each of the two of Epkinly's target indications (DLBCL and FL). This number will increase as the company moves from the third line of therapy to the second line of therapy and possibly the first line of therapy. For example, in the case of 1L DLBCL, the population is over 70,000 patients. Therefore, even if Epkinly takes a small percentage of the overall market in this space, the company has the potential for a multibillion dollar peak sales opportunity.

The gynecologic oncology market for Rina-S is equally large. There are over 100,000 patients with endometrial cancer in the major geographies who receive their initial treatment for this indication. The available treatments today for these patients, after they fail on platinum-based therapies and/or immunotherapies, are very limited. Therefore, an effective ADC in this setting would fill a very large hole. Although there are several competing products to Rina-S in this space, the amount of unmet need in this space is so large that the market is expected to be able to support multiple blockbusters. However, in order for this to occur, the product must allow for long-term dosing without causing side effects. Rina-S has the potential to be a competitor in this regard due to its lack of ocular toxicity.

Future Outlook

The value proposition of Genmab has changed. Genmab can now be considered a company with a proven discovery engine, a fully funded pipeline of wholly owned assets that are now in late stage clinical trials, and a leadership team that has shown to deliver on their expectations. I believe in the coming years, Genmab will be valued no longer as solely a royalty generating company, but instead as a standalone major player in the field of oncology. For those investors who are willing to see this transition through, there is a tremendous amount of potential for long-term growth. The science behind Genmab’s development is sound, the financials are solid, and the current strategy is working well.

data.title: Carlyle Group: The Underlying Thesis For Investment Management Underperformance In 2026E
data.description:
In this article, I'll be updating my thesis on the Carlyle Group (CG), a global investment firm with over $400B in AUM. Between three segments of credit, solutions, and PE, the company has an attractive set of exposures at the right price. I have, historically, been investing in the company and saw a very good return prior to selling off. Since my sale, the company first went "down" a bit more in October/November of last year, before bouncing back up to where we're now seeing a decent short-term return. Relative to the return I made when I sold, I view this outperformance as negligible. The larger perspective is more important for me at this time, and as I detailed in my previous article, which you can find here, there are reasons that I see that imply this company as being materially and objectively overvalued for the context of the macro we are in today.

I will be justifying that statement in the article - objectively overvalued - because that is a strong statement to make. In this case, I believe it to be accurate.

Carlyle Group, as many of its European peers that I cover, is an attractive operator at a good price. While the US-side representation in the PE sector is much larger than in Europe, we do have some companies specializing in what it does - such as EQT, CVC Capital, KKR & Co, and Ardian, the latter out of France.

Still, it's probably an accurate statement to say that most are more familiar with US-based peers to Carlyle, such as Blackstone (BX), Apollo Global, and even financial "monoliths" that have their own business in this field, such as BlackRock.

However, there is an underlying story here as to why these companies are not good investments in this situation and valuation.

That is the story I'll be telling, to pick up from my previous article.

Carlyle Group - Performance is good, but the prospects will be dictated by global development

My forecast for the 3Q25 period, which my previous article was a preview of, was not wrong. The company did see considerable challenges, and the near-term AEPS growth forecast has been downgraded by about 3-4%, with a longer-term forecast characterized by the same sort of uncertainty that, for this company, results in over 50% negative forecast accuracy, even with a 10-20% margin of error.

In short, the company is volatile and hard to predict. If you're investing here, you're "betting" on outperformance or hitting targets, which, if we look at the historical accuracy, is a courageous bet.

There are also plenty of indicators that the PE industry as a whole, which is one of the company's biggest sectors, is facing a downturn. Funds have been facing sub-par returns for some time, and the industry, which has been picking up tens of thousands of investments, particularly in IT/AI, has had trouble divesting these investments despite a very solid market and high valuations.

This so-called slowdown in exits is putting the brakes on the entire business model that these companies operate with. The investor hesitancy that the market is struggling with, in my opinion, is a clear indicator of an overvalued market not only in PE, but in general. These extended holding periods for a company that operates based on entry and exits cause significantly lower returns if they persist for longer. The hesitancy itself means that the company struggles with liquidity issues, because it results in less new funding for its new PE investment funds. These financial conditions with similar exit opportunities mean that the foundational model for these PE firms is currently not as great as some would argue.

PE firms had a great time when debt was cheap - that is no longer the case. The company's primary method of investing, the so-called LBO, is now significantly more expensive. This, in turn, discourages IPOs, which means that the companies, as mentioned above, have difficulty exiting an investment.

The bearish scenario that I see here is that many of these PE firms could be left "sitting" with investments that are either not that profitable, or less profitable than they'd like, but without the option of fulfilling their mandate and strategy - namely, selling at an attractive RoR. The required returns for a PE company are extremely high, meaning that the returns that these companies need to get can cause them to take significant risks. A longer holding period for these investments for a PE company is not a good thing. The typical holding period is 4-5 years - we're now looking at 6 or more.

The above-mentioned challenges are part reality, part "if"/risks. But if these realities turn out to move in the direction I expect, then this will result in a market where it's far harder to price these PE-oriented investment companies, even if CG has operations in other subsectors as well. This uncertainty needs to be priced into the valuation/discount of a company like CG - that's where I believe the market is not doing a "right" job.

The 3Q25 results were lower than analyst forecasts in several areas, which led to a drop in share price on earnings day. The total decrease YoY was over 12%, and a 20.7% miss against the forecast - a clear sign of the company's forecast inaccuracy and volatility. These results can be weighted vis-à-vis the CEO's earnings call positivity about deal-making being easier.

However, first of all, that's something I'd need to see in results - and even if the company manages to hit forecasts for 2025E, 2026-2027E is likely to be a different "beast" with the current set of volatile macroeconomics. What worries me the most about 3Q25 is the missed sales expectations - these confirm my worry that there's something near-term potentially flawed about the PE company approach that needs to be re-evaluated - at least in the near term.

The valuation for the company typically does not imply a premium - in fact, the average is below 13x P/E. So when this company trades close to 16x, meaningfully above the 20-year average, I see an increase in risk that I view as unattractive.

Valuation for Carlyle Group - Not necessarily attractive even if the company's results materialize

Let's start with the bullish thesis. Bulls would forecast the company at 15-16x P/E, about 10-22% above the average for the 5-20 year average, with expected AEPS growth of about 11% per year from efficiencies, results, and stability in sales as well as other areas. I would characterize such forecasts as somewhat "rosy" - too rosy. The highest this company has ever managed at any time average, is 13.2x P/E.

But even in the event of a 15-16x P/E, you're not getting a market-beating rate of return for this company.

And if we forecast at about 12x P/E, the company's annualized RoR without the 2.16% dividend is an unattractive 1.5% per year.

So, in any scenario, even those that the bullish analysts consider to be relevant here, the annualized rate of return based on a double-digit AEPS growth is less than 12% per year, inclusive of the dividend.

This is the justification for why I say the company is objectively overvalued/not attractive here. Investing with market-beating returns would require the company to outperform in such a way that would be 50-70% above the typical averages in a market where many believe that the business model the company uses for at least half its operations is not as attractive as it was a few years back.

With this as justification, I view the company as unattractive in the near-to-medium term. I looked at my share price target of $50/share and fair value target estimates and considerations, and saw no other choice than to downgrade based on this.

I now view CG as a PT of $48/share, with a fair value of about $70/share - and that's expecting a double-digit AEPS growth, meaning that this is actually a relatively bullish expectation.

I therefore view Carlyle Group as unattractive with a "HOLD" rating, reiterated for the 2026E period. Remember that I am not negative on Carlyle. Since I was positive, and actually held a position in the company, Carlyle has significantly outperformed as per my expectations. If I had timed my exit better, the returns would have been even better - but this does not, in my view, make the company attractive now.

Alternatives to Carlyle would include companies in the same field, provided they were attractive. However, peers like Blackstone all come with the same sort of trends, risks, and downsides. The difference with Blackstone is that it trades at a significantly higher premium, despite an even higher forecast uncertainty than the Carlyle Group.

It makes the peers, and the entire sector, in my view, something that you probably don't want to invest in here.

I would view other sectors as far more attractive, and would not view the company as a potential "BUY" here.

Risks & Considerations

There's plenty of volatility here that could drive short- or even medium-term earnings negative or positive. That's also the biggest risk against my neutral thesis, but my conviction is that we won't see a recovery to above $68/share, nor is it justified if we do. That's also why I bought it when the company was cheap.

By paying attention and buying during tough times or when the company was at low, sub-10x multiples, you could have generated long-term 13-14% annualized RoR, and that has the potential to turn capital into substantial wealth, given the right amount of patience and attention to that valuation.

A continuation of the market inflation remains the core risk against my neutral/negative thesis for the company, and it's entirely possible we'll see years of further inflation before a downturn begins. In such a scenario, the company is obviously still attractive. But I do not consider this to be likely.

Even with the latest set of results, I do not view this to be a materially attractive risk/reward ratio or situation, especially with the increase in geopolitical tension(s) we've been seeing over the past 2 months. While tension and instability can be fertile ground for attractive investing, I believe there will be a correction not only in this sector, but across the markets, and this makes this company unattractive as an investment here.

The thesis is, now as of 2026E, as follows.

Thesis

Carlyle is at risk (Now, as of 2026E and 3Q25A, validated with slower sales and underperformance) of seeing a slowdown in business due to an increased valuation and global volatility. At this time and at this price, following my exit from the company a few weeks back, I no longer see any worthwhile upside to investing in.

Between risks for a slowdown, between the company's volatility and the entire sector's volatility, I would view any investment here as unfavorable compared to other sectors that are much more attractive.

CG is a "HOLD" here. A price target that I would consider attractive for investment based on my goals would be around $48/share - though every investor, of course, needs to look at their own targets, goals, and strategies. I got a good return from this investment, and if we see a sub-$48/share price, I would be happy to go back in.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of $48/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Capricor Therapeutics: A High Risk/High Reward Name
data.description:
Shares of Duchenne muscular dystrophy concern Capricor Therapeutics, Inc. (CAPR) more than tripled on December 3, 2025 after releasing seemingly approvable Phase 3 disease progression data. The HOPE-3 results, including a 54% slowing of upper limb performance deterioration versus placebo (p=0.029) at 12 months, should help deramiocel overcome a July 2025 CRL from the FDA. With an opportunity estimated between $7 billion and $27 billion by 2030 and commercial partnerships in place for the U.S. and Japan, the rise in Capricor’s market cap merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Capricor Therapeutics, Inc. is a San Diego-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of cell therapy and exosome-based treatments against diseases with high unmet needs. That said, the company is advancing one serious clinical program, allogeneic (donor-derived) cell therapy deramiocel for the treatment of cardiomyopathy associated with Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD). Capricor was formed in 2005 and went public in 2013, when it reverse merged into failed cardiovascular concern Nile Therapeutics, with its first trade transacted at $21.60 per share, after giving effect to a 1-for-10 reverse stock split. Shares of CAPR trade around $25.50 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of just over $1.3 billion.

Deramiocel

The company’s raison d’etre is deramiocel, an allogenic cell therapy comprised of caridosphere-derived cells, which are “a unique population of cardiac cells derived from healthy donor hearts… [exerting] their therapeutic effect through immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, pro angiogenic, and anti-fibrotic mechanisms – primarily mediated by secreted exosomes enriched with bioactive microRNAs.” In other words, they are connective tissue cells designed to secrete exosomes, which reprogram pathogen-devouring macrophages to combat inflammation to fight cardiomyopathy, the leading cause of death in DMD patients. Delivered intravenously once every three months for one year, these non-coding RNA constructs offer an alternative approach to the exon-skipping therapies pioneered by Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) or gene replacement therapy currently under clinical investigation by Solid Biosciences (SLDB) and others.

DMD Marketplace & Regulatory Environment

Made famous by the Jerry Lewis Labor Day Telethons, DMD is a monogenic, X-linked (male) recessive disease caused by mutations that encode for the dystrophin protein. Essential for the structural integrity and function of muscle cells involved in walking, breathing, and cardiac function, dystrophin proteins are reduced or absent in DMD patients due to mutations in the dystrophin gene. Symptoms such as muscle weakness and wasting present in the first few years of life, with most boys wheelchair-bound by their teenage years, followed by progression to respiratory and cardiac failure, with nearly all sufferers dead by the age of 30. This dreadful malady afflicts ~12,000 to ~15,000 in the U.S. and ~150,000 to ~200,000 globally.

DMD can be subcategorized based on the faulty or misaligned sections (exons) of genetic code that are responsible for the disease. As stated earlier, Sarepta developed three conditionally approved ‘patches’ that promote skipping of exons 45, 51, and 53 of the dystrophin gene, which account for ~29% of the DMD population. That said, Solid Biosciences is attempting to cover the entire board – including the ~30% that may not be amenable to any exon skipping – with its synthetic transgene therapy SGT-003, delivering not a patch but a whole new gene to the patient. A BLA filing with the FDA is anticipated as early as 2H26. Since Sarepta received what some would call a controversial approval for its first exon skipping therapy (Exondys 51) in 2016, the FDA has evolved to emphasize disease progression measures such as performance of upper limb (PUL) and left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) as much as biomarker data such as dystrophin production.

Rounding out the competitive clinical landscape in exon skipping approaches for DMD, Dyne Therapeutics (DYN) expects to file a BLA for its exon 51 skipping (13% of DMD population) therapy z-rostudirsen. Novartis (NVS) ponied up $12 billion for Avidity Biosciences in part to obtain oligonucleotide therapy del-zota that treats DMD amenable to exon 44 skipping.

As for gene therapy approaches, Regenxbio’s (RGNX) RGX-202 demonstrated functional improvements over historical norms in four patients covering the Phase 1/2 portion of its Phase 1/2/3 study (AFFINITY DUCHENNE). Pivotal enrollment is now complete, with top-line data anticipated in 1H26, followed by a mid-2026 accelerated approval filing, assuming positive results. Also, privately held Genethon is advancing a DMD gene therapy (GNT 0004) into the pivotal portion of a slow-moving Phase 1/2/3 trial in France and the UK. There is also early stage candidates from Entrada (TRDA) and Insmed (INSM).

Depending on the research service employed, DMD treatments generated revenue anywhere between $2.2 billion and $3.5 billion in 2024, but that significantly understates the opportunity if a truly game-changing therapy covering all sub-categories of the disease emerged. With ~150,000 to ~200,000 afflicted worldwide, some market research firms estimate the true opportunity at anywhere between ~$7 billion (Evaluate Pharma) and ~$27 billion (ResearchAndMarkets.com) by 2030.

Deramiocel Regulatory Saga

Capricor appeared poised to attain approval for deramiocel when the FDA accepted its BLA for DMD-cardiomyopathy in March 2025, assigning it a PDUFA date of August 31, 2025. The application was supported by data from the company’s 20-patient Phase 2 trial (HOPE-2) and 13-patient open label extension study (HOPE-2 OLE) that demonstrated statistically significant improvements in cardiac function – most notably a 99% slowing of disease progression from baseline (as measured by LVEF) and 6.4% absolute improvement versus historical comparator (p=0.008) in ten patients at year two. The FDA disagreed, issuing a complete response letter (CRL) in July 2025 stating that Capricor “failed to demonstrate efficacy for its prespecified primary efficacy endpoint,” which was the 22-item PUL version 2.0. (PUL v2.0) survey. Although the company strongly disagreed with the agency’s logic – believing that it had achieved statistical significance for PUL v2.0 – investors fled for the exits, with shares of CAPR falling 33% to $7.64 per share in the subsequent trading session, eventually bottoming out at $4.30 on November 25, 2025.

The saving grace for Capricor is that it was also conducting a much larger (106-patient), randomized 1:1, placebo-controlled Phase 3 trial (HOPE-3), which produced supportive data on December 3, 2025. On the primary endpoint of PUL v2.0, deramiocel demonstrated a 54% slowing of upper limb performance deterioration versus placebo (p=0.029) at 12 months. It also achieved a 91% slowing of disease progression in key secondary endpoint LVEF (n=83) (p=0.041). Armed with these data, the company expects to submit a response to the CRL, setting it up for approval sometime in 2026.

In response to these Phase 3 results, shares of CAPR rocketed 371% to $29.96 in the subsequent trading session. The shares have fallen back by some 15% since.

Commercial Partnership

Assuming approval, Capricor will receive an $80 million milestone payment from Nippon Shinyaku (NPNKF), which secured the domestic and Japanese distribution rights to deramiocel in 2022. Furthermore, the company is eligible to receive domestic commercial milestones up to $605 million as well as 30% to 50% of the U.S. product revenue. In Japan, Capricor is eligible for potential milestones from Nippon Shinyaku totaling $89 million and double-digit royalties. The company is on the hook for product supply and attendant costs.

European partnership negotiations are ongoing.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Management quickly turned the rally into a liquidity event, raising net proceeds of just over $160 million at $25 per share, assuming greenshoe exercise. Added to its cash and marketable securities position of $98.6 million on September 30, 2025, Capricor has secured an operating runway through 2027, before giving effect to any potential milestone payments or a potential receipt of a priority review voucher.

Despite the regulatory rollercoaster ride, sell-side analysts have been unanimously positive throughout 2025, as can be seen above. On average, they expect deramiocel to generate royalties and milestones of $127 million in FY2026, accelerating to over $600 million by 2030.

Verdict:

There should be enough evidence from HOPE-3 to push deramiocel across the FDA finish line. Capricor’s current valuation of ~$1.1 billion net of cash would appear rich given that it has sold the domestic commercial rights away to its sole clinical therapy – if you exclude an exosome-derived Sars-CoV-2 vaccine in Phase 1 development. However, the terms of that arrangement are quite generous for the company. With the caveat that the downside is extreme if the FDA rejects deramiocel again, the substantial premium in Capricor’s options would make it a solid covered call candidate if the liquidity in the options was slightly better. That said, I just took a small position in the stock using the June $23 call strikes for a net debit of $17.50 a share last month. For speculative and aggressive investors, that is how I would initiate a small position in the shares. For more risk-averse investors, the stock should have too much volatility in the quarters ahead to be considered for investment.

data.title: IWL: High-Quality Large-Cap Play With A Few Advantages Over IVV
data.description:
With today's note, I would like to initiate coverage of the iShares Russell Top 200 ETF (IWL), an exchange-traded fund that I believe could be considered a decent alternative to the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), so a Buy rating looks proper.

What I like about IWL is its outperformance versus IVV since 2009 and, despite anecdotal evidence suggesting that its smaller portfolio should have higher volatility and, consequently, less appealing risk-adjusted returns, quite convincing risk metrics as well. The factor mix speaks in favor of it too, as it has mostly stronger quality than IVV and larger exposure to growth, the factor duo I favor for 2026.

Of course, a Buy rating does not mean IWL is superior to IVV in all aspects and does not have any vulnerabilities. It has quite a few, key among them being its expense ratio of 15 bps, 5x that of IVV, as well as the maximum drawdown that it delivered in 2022 that gives food for thought about how its decline might look if the bear scenario were to repeat, for example, if the pace of interest rate cuts disappoints or any other powerful downside catalyst emerges.

IWL Key Facts

  • Inception: September 22, 2009
  • AUM: $2.14 billion, as of January 8
  • Expense Ratio: 0.15%
  • 10-Year Annualized Total Return (January 2016–December 2025 period): 15.56% vs. 14.79% of IVV

IWL Strategy

In my view, IWL's strategy is quite unsophisticated and straightforward but still a rather potent one, which is confirmed by returns.

As we know from the iShares website, the lynchpin of IWL's strategy is the Russell Top 200 Index. As explained in the prospectus,

The Underlying Index is a subset of the Russell 3000 Index, which measures the performance of the broad U.S. equity market, as defined by Russell. The Underlying Index is a float-adjusted capitalization-weighted index of equity securities issued by the approximately 199 largest issuers in the Russell 3000 Index.

Here, an essential remark is that to qualify for the Russell 3000, and obviously for the Russell 1000 and Russell Top 200 indices as well, there is no need for a company to be profitable, which is in contrast to the S&P 500's famous rule requiring turning a profit, among other things. And Tesla's (TSLA) example is the one I frequently cite when discussing the indices' differences. The company qualified for the Russell 1000 index in September 2010, but its ascent into the S&P 500 was delayed by more than a decade and finally happened, as I recall, with a great deal of buzz in the financial media, only in December 2020. So the bottom line here is that it is reasonable to expect some portfolio and performance differences between IWL and IVV driven by this methodological nuance.

IWL Performance

IWL's performance story is notable for a number of reasons.

  • First, it managed to beat IVV in ten out of sixteen full calendar years it has in the books, clocking a 14.67% annualized return for the October 2009–December 2025 period.
  • Second, and more importantly, its volatility was adequate, so despite being much less diversified than the S&P 500 ETF, it still delivered higher risk-adjusted returns.

So let us zoom in on these issues, starting with its performance since inception. Here, we see that a leaner portfolio and focus on bellwethers secured IWL's massive outperformance versus IVV and the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Nevertheless, just to contextualize, I have added the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), which has an even more compact portfolio and less interest in sectors other than tech. Beating it, of course, was a task that IWL, IVV, and IWB were not even remotely close to accomplishing. On a positive side, their volatility was much less pronounced, and the declines in stressful conditions were also less deep.

The next subjects of our analysis are, as usual, the annualized returns and a few selected risk metrics, and here things become a bit trickier.

The issue is that, in general, concentration translates into higher volatility. But for IWL, this was not the case, and its standard deviation was even lower than IVV's over the October 2009–December 2025 period, at 14.12%. Moreover, it captured 97.39% of IVV's downside, which is also relatively low.

So what we see is not only a 41 bps higher annualized return (secured, among other things, by its 2023–2025 outperformance streak) but also stronger risk-adjusted returns, including the Sharpe, Sortino, and Treynor ratios.

At the same time, we do see one problem, and it is IWL's maximum drawdown of 24.7% (September 2022), deeper than those of IWB and IVV. The growthier QQQ did worse, though.

IWL Portfolio

As of January 7, there were 200 equities in the IWL portfolio, with the lion's share of the net assets (about 44.6%), quite expectedly, allocated to the trillion-dollar league, and with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) sitting at the very top. While this league is the core part of the IVV portfolio and essentially the top driver of its gains, its allocation to it is smaller, about 38.7% as of the same date.

Company Ticker Weight In IWL, as of January 7 Weight in IVV, as of January 7 Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of January 8
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) 8.71% 7.76% Information Technology 4,595.18
Apple Inc. (AAPL) 7.47% 6.50% Information Technology 3,846.73
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) 7.06% 6.07% Information Technology 3,593.33
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) 4.56% 3.97% Consumer Discretionary 2,582.33
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) 3.69% 3.16% Communication 3,887.77
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 3.12% 2.74% Information Technology 1,628.63
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) 3.00% 2.53% Communication 3,887.77
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) 2.79% 2.39% Communication 1,635.04
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) 2.40% 2.05% Consumer Discretionary 1,434.79
Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) 1.80% 1.54% Financials 1,070.73
Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) 1.75% 1.49% Health Care 992.16

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

As we can see from the table showing the key ten companies in the IWL portfolio, the ETF is overweight in all these names compared to IVV, which is one of the reasons why its factor mix looks stronger, a fact we will be discussing shortly.

As to the sector mix, my point is that the differences between IVV and IWL appear subtle but still discernible.

For instance, compared to IVV, IWL is overweight in only two sectors, namely communication and IT, while underweight in others except for health care, to which the ETF's exposures are on par.

However, the differences between IWL and the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM) (which is, in essence, in most aspects similar to QQQ except for the expense ratio and AUM), another Magnificent Seven-heavy play, which has much larger exposure to IT, are more pronounced. This in part explains why QQQM's factor mix is stronger in terms of growth and quality.

IWL Factor Mix

Speaking about the factor mix, the IWL portfolio looks in many ways ahead of that of IVV. Nevertheless, with the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF added to the comparison, it looks decent yet qualifies for second place only.

It is worth giving a bit more attention to IWL's main advantages here.

  • Its weighted average growth metrics, as well as its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Growth grade or higher, are stronger than those of the IVV portfolio.
  • Exposures to the quality and momentum factors also look more appealing.

However, the GARP characteristics, those that I believe deserve attention in today's high-priced market, are where IWL does not stand out decisively.

I have calculated three metrics, namely the PEG ratio and two hybrid ratios, and the story they tell is not completely clear.

Metric IWL QQQM IVV
PEG Ratio 1.183 1.212 1.231
Price/Sales divided by forward revenue growth rate 0.582 0.567 0.578
EV/EBITDA divided by forward EBITDA growth rate 1.054 1.003 1.088

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

  • On the one hand, IWL has the lowest PEG compared to QQQM and IVV (the differences are minor though), while its EV/EBITDA-to-growth ratio is lower than IVV's, which indicates a slight GARP tilt.
  • On the other hand, its Price/Sales-to-Growth ratio is the highest in the group.

Final Thoughts

In sum, as I like both IWL's performance story and factor exposures, I am initiating the coverage with a Buy rating. But it is unwise to expect a smooth ride for it or any other growth-heavy ETF this year. The growth premia they carry are very sensitive to monetary policy and macro data, plus any deviations from AI hyperscalers' growth trajectory can cause quite a lot of harm to their valuations, and quickly. And compared to a better diversified IVV, top-heavy IWL is at a disadvantage here.

data.title: Dollar General Corp: Still Attractive At This Valuation
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Dollar General Corp (DG) in September ’25, with my key thesis being that DG’s fundamentals have improved; there has been clear momentum in demand, and the new digital levers are working. The “trade-in” dynamic from higher-income shoppers is getting more apparent, and DG showed us that it can continue to capture discretionary purchase demand. Combined with confidence from management (via their revised guide), I believe the stock still looks attractive at this valuation.

The Trade-In Thesis

The key debate I had for DG was that the increasing mix of higher-income shoppers was becoming a trend. To my surprise, this was just a trend; it is an accelerating trend, which makes me think this could really become a structural tailwind. Just for reference, DG managed to inch out a 2.5% y/y increase in customer traffic despite the entire retail sector facing consumer spending weakness. And importantly, it was confirmed that the traffic increase came from all income cohorts (low, middle, and high).

The most important data point is that households earning >$100,000 were the fastest-growing customer cohort in the latest quarter. I have given my thoughts on this higher-income “trade-in” impact before, and I believe the framing shouldn’t just be that the consumers are looking to save costs (i.e., looking for value purchases). Surely there is a group of high-income customers that are shifting to DG because of that reason, but I believe there is a group that switched to DG because they want to. Now, this is an entirely different framing because this type of demand is stickier and suggests that DG is successfully expanding its total addressable market. The way I see it, when a six-figure earner is not just buying essentials but also buying from discretionary categories (can be seen by the 7.6% comp in the 'Value Valley' segment, more on this below), it proves that DG is meeting a certain standard of preference, not just necessity.

The Value Valley Proves Discretionary Demand Isn't Dead

The fact that DG’s The Value Valley assortment delivered a 7.6% comp tells us that discretionary spending is not entirely dead. Consumers still want discretionary products; they are only more focused on price. You could argue that because The Value Valley prices its products at $1, hence, there is demand. I would push back to say that pricing is only part of the equation. It is DG’s execution that made this work. If the product assortment didn't resonate with the consumer's current taste and needs, it doesn’t matter what prices DG sets; the products wouldn’t sell. Within this aspect, there is “having the right product assortment” and “having the right experience.” The former is important, but the latter is what makes DGs different.

It all anchors on the "treasure hunt" dynamic, which I think is the key to purchase conversion because consumers believe they have “discovered” a high-value item for a low price. And this strategy really only works when the perceived risk is low. When you ask a consumer to pay $20 for a home décor item, the reaction is, "Do I really need this?" But at $1, that question is unlikely to pop up in the mind because even if it turns out to be a "bad" purchase, it doesn’t really hurt the wallet. This opens up the shopping occasion, what I call “experimental shopping,” wherein consumers simply buy it and figure out later whether it’s worth it. That effectively solves the hurdle in purchase conversion today, where consumers are leaning towards saying "no" to buying discretionary products (because they want to save money).

Guidance Confirms the Turnaround Is Real

Perhaps the most bullish signal is that management has raised FY25 guidance with confidence. The EPS guide was lifted to $6.3 to $6.50, and same-store sales [SSS] growth to 2.5% to 2.7%, from $5.8 to $6.3, and 2.1% to 2.6% previously. This is a very aggressive upwards revision, which tells me that management is seeing momentum continue into Q4. Note that the guide was upgraded on 4th December, and the DG fiscal quarter ends on 31st October, which means management already has ~5 weeks of Q4 data. For them to raise the guidance, it must at least show that demand has not weakened. 3rd party tracking data also points to acceleration through Q4, which gives comfort.

This upgrade also shifts the stock sentiment because for the last two years, the stock’s narrative has been mostly about negative earnings revisions. The consensus that followed management earnings got disappointed. But with this upgrade, and if DG really meets that guide, the market should start to give more credit to management’s guidance, which will be helpful to anchor near-term expectations (i.e., there will be less surprise/disappointment that could swing the share price).

Valuation

Valuation-wise, DG now trades at 20x forward earnings, which is where I believe it should at least trade at. I still think there is room for multiples to go higher if earnings growth can accelerate to the low teens. The FY25 guide already implies adj. EPS to grow ~10%, as a reminder. But even if multiples don’t expand, a retailer that is doing very well despite the retail environment, expanding its targeted customer spectrum, and having a solid strategy to capture even discretionary purchases can grow adj. EPS at 10% and should continue to trade at 20x (similar to Dollar Tree (DLTR)’s current valuation and earnings growth expectations). Compounding FY24 adj. EPS of $5.92 by 10% over the next three years equates to ~$7.9 adj. EPS. 20x that equates to a share price of ~$158.

Investment Risk

One nuance that I see as a mini red flag is that management guided for new store returns of 16% to 17%, a drop from the historical >20% standard. While 16% to 17% is still attractive, this does suggest that growth via new stores is seeing diminishing returns. If the pace of decline accelerates, the DG growth runway could be shorter than expected.

Conclusion

I maintain a positive view on DG because the improved fundamentals have gotten clearer, showing the expansion of the customer base, proving discretionary demand still exists, and also improved guidance. While store-level returns bear monitoring, the current earnings trajectory still looks healthy.

data.title: Fiserv: Accumulating Through A Transition, Not Timing A Bottom
data.description:
The fall in Fiserv (FISV) after a dismal Q3 2025 guidance opens up more than a speculative window to initiate fresh positions. I feel the current levels are more about market momentum on the downside that could drag share prices below what fundamentals indicate. This fall also appears to be around a credibility reset (with management changes also hampering sentiment).

But this is not a broken business. Valuations today, as my DCF analysis indicates, are pricing in far too much pessimism, like low single-digit growth forever at permanently impaired margins. The real case is that of a potential margin trough, not a permanent regime change. Cash flow should remain intact, and EPS growth should resume after a reinvestment phase.

My Reading of the Corrections

First of all, Fiserv's offering is not a cyclical fintech product. Its payments and transactions infrastructure products are mission-critical systems with high switching costs. The business reaps the repeatability of client relationships and a recurring revenue and ends up with strong free cash flow over a cycle. Such businesses are rarely broken unless the core franchise is impaired.

That said, corrections were legitimate and attributable to three key overlapping shocks that stand out - although the extent of damage appears overdone. Argentina normalization hit growth optics after strong margin and growth trends in 2023-24. Once rates normalized, comparables became tougher, and growth and margin trends suffered. This is not deterioration of the business - only a slowdown of a significant tailwind.

The second factor is confidence altering, in addition to the mechanical shock by itself. The prior 10–12% organic growth guidance was reported to be based on unrealistic assumptions around non-Argentina business growth, aggressive sales productivity and deal conversions, and an extrapolation of peak pricing and revenue levers that drove much of the growth in 2023-2024.

Thirdly, signs of over-earning emerged as some margins were supported by deferred investments and some growth was driven by tactical pushes that do not lend themselves to growth sustainability in the long term. However, Fiserv has now chosen to reinvest in technology and service and shown a willingness to front-load some short term earnings pains to rebuild durable growth in the longer haul.

My reading of the transition strategy for Fiserv is that even if a turnaround in numbers is not likely in the next quarter or two, the bar has been set lower, which should manage investor expectations far better. Core assets still show healthy signs. Clover GPV still grew double digits (excluding gateway conversion noise). Issuing continues to gain share, debit volumes are healthy, and embedded finance platforms are expanding TAM. Importantly, there has been no material client attrition reported or structural damage (like, say, tech obsolescence). Repurchases have continued (7m shares in Q3 with 49m shares still left pre-authorized if the shares dip further). The free cash flow outlook has also not been altered, which limits long-term and materially deeper drawdowns from here on.

And then there's no change in the free cash flow generation capabilities of the company. And if it's run right for the long term, the conversion stays very, very attractive. There is absolutely no change in our capital management plan.

A Valuation Check

For Fiserv, the aim of my DCF is not precision. I simply want to show that under new growth and cash flow assumptions, it is pessimistically priced. According to guidance now, the free cash flow for the full year is expected to be $4.25b in a heavy investment year. That is my starting point for the DCF model. This is a step down from current TTM levels by around 10% and already assumes higher capex, lower margins (has already started to impact the FCF trajectory in 2025). Guidance obviously also already accounts for no tailwinds from Argentina.

According to management's revised conservative guidance, growth in 2026 is likely to be low single digit, before it reverts to mid-single digit growth beyond 2026-27. I assume similar growth in free cash flow, given Fiserv's history of efficient cash conversion. Importantly, this assumption does not rely on margin expansion or cost efficiencies and is, in fact, in line with management's resilient free cash flow generation outlook.

Finally, the DCF uses a WACC of 9.5% for a mature fintech infrastructure player with high recurring revenue, but acknowledges some execution risk. A terminal growth rate of 3% is assumed. The fair value based on these revised and relatively conservative assumptions shows a fair value of above $75 for Fiserv, which is the basis of my overshot fundamentals claim. DCFs are generally assumption-heavy, and changing the terminal rate to 2.5% shows a fair value closer to today's prices. However, as stated at the start of the analysis, I aim to show how well-supported current prices are and most of the pessimism is well baked in, if not overdone.

Patient Buy Opportunity

My DCF assumptions are in line with analyst expectations reported in Seeking Alpha. These revisions have now been downgraded after the Q3 guidance and will await fresh data and developments over the next couple of earnings calls to reward or punish the stock further.

In the upcoming quarterly results and commentary, I will watch out for free cash flow stability even as Fiserv goes through a capex cycle. This is more critical in my view than margin troughs, which are expected (although surprises could still open up further downside). On the business health, continued Clover GPV growth and client retention signals should help stabilize worries.

However, as my analysis finds, current prices are reasonable to start accumulating, given current information. If new information surfaces in Q4 2025 or Q1 2026, the thesis will be revised - particularly if free cash flow conversion shows structural deterioration. If not, any further drawdown should be used to accumulate through the next couple of quarters. Investors have to recognize that expected returns on this position are more likely to show up in 2027 and beyond - this is not a Q4 '25 or Q1 '26 trade. Any surprises relative to what may prove to be conservative guidance, could expedite rerating.

Risks to the Thesis

The biggest risk to the thesis, as I have outlined earlier, is pressure on FCF that is assumed to stay normalized, particularly as Fiserv is entering a reinvestment phase. If elevated capex adds to structural changes to operating costs, it could hamper assumptions of the baseline FCF expectations close to current levels (if not subsequent growth), making the trough more structural than temporary.

Execution risks should also be acknowledged. Any delay in product rollout or service quality issues through transition could limit growth to low single-digit levels. In such a case, the reversion to mid-single-digit growth assumed in my DCF becomes optimistic.

Thirdly, margin recovery may take longer than expected if the transition plays out over years in terms of higher investments - although my DCF model does not rely on margin expansion, it needs margin stability. Sentiment and multiple risks also may remain a drag on the share till credibility (through execution) resurfaces.
